sn3.1:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
sn3.1:1.3: Then King Pasenadi Kosala went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, exchanged courteous greetings with him.
sn3.1:1.4: After this exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to the Blessed One:
sn3.1:1.5: “Now then, does Master Gotama claim, ‘I have awakened to the unexcelled right self-awakening’?”
sn3.1:1.6: “If, great king, one speaking rightly could say of anyone, ‘He has awakened to the unexcelled right self-awakening,’ one could rightly say that of me.
sn3.1:1.7: For I, great king, have awakened to the unexcelled right self-awakening.”
sn3.1:2.1: “But Master Gotama, those contemplatives & brahmans, each with his group, each with his community, each the teacher of his group, an honored leader, well-regarded by people at large—i.e.,
sn3.1:2.2: Pūraṇa Kassapa, Makkhali Gosāla, Ajita Kesakambalin, Pakudha Kaccāyana, Sañjaya Velaṭṭhaputta, and the Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta:
sn3.1:2.3: Even they, when I asked them whether they claimed to have awakened to the unexcelled right self-awakening, didn’t make that claim.
sn3.1:2.4: So who is Master Gotama to do so when he is still young & newly gone forth?”
sn3.1:3.1: “There are these four things, great king, that shouldn’t be despised & disparaged for being young.
sn3.1:3.3: A noble warrior, great king, shouldn’t be despised & disparaged for being young.
sn3.1:3.6: And a monk shouldn’t be despised & disparaged for being young.
sn3.1:3.7: These are the four things that shouldn’t be despised & disparaged for being young.”
sn3.1:4.1: That is what the Blessed One said.
sn3.1:4.2: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
sn3.1:5.1: “You shouldn’t look down on a noble warrior of consummate birth,
sn3.1:5.2: a high-born prince of great status.
sn3.1:6.1: For it’s possible that this lord of human beings, this noble warrior,
sn3.1:6.4: come down harshly with his royal might.
sn3.1:7.1: a serpent you meet in village or wilderness:
sn3.1:7.3: You shouldn’t look down on —for being young—
sn3.1:8.2: snake slithers along with vibrant colors,
sn3.1:8.3: it may someday burn the fool,
sn3.1:9.1: a blaze that feeds on many things,
sn3.1:9.2: a flame with its blackened trail:
sn3.1:9.3: You shouldn’t look down on —for being young—
sn3.1:10.2: becoming a great mass of flame,
sn3.1:10.3: it may someday burn the fool,
sn3.1:11.1: When a fire burns down a forest
sn3.1:11.3: the shoots there take birth once more
sn3.1:11.4: with the passage of days & nights.
sn3.1:12.1: But if a monk, his virtue consummate,
sn3.1:12.3: you won’t acquire sons or cattle
sn3.1:12.4: nor will your heirs enjoy wealth.
sn3.1:13.2: out of regard for his own good,
sn3.1:13.3: should always show due respect for a serpent, a fire,
sn3.1:13.4: a noble warrior with high status,
sn3.1:14.1: When this was said, King Pasenadi Kosala said to the Blessed One:
sn3.1:14.3: Just as if he were to place upright what was overturned, to reveal what was hidden, to show the way to one who was lost, or to carry a lamp into the dark so that those with eyes could see forms, in the same way has the Blessed One—through many lines of reasoning—made the Dhamma clear.
sn3.1:14.4: I go to the Blessed One for refuge, to the Dhamma, and to the Saṅgha of monks.
sn3.1:14.5: May the Blessed One remember me as a lay follower who has gone to him for refuge, from this day forward, for life.”
